name: Lin Method (Square-1)
description: An alternative Square-1 method inspired by blockbuilding concepts. The Lin method takes a fundamentally different approach from the standard Vandenbergh method by focusing on pairing blocks during cubeshape and solving in fewer, more integrated steps. It pairs edges and corners during the cubeshape phase, then uses a CSP (Cubeshape Parity) aware approach to solve the last layer. The method offers lower move counts but requires more advanced planning and recognition.

steps.name: Cubeshape + Pair Blocks
steps.description: Restore the cube shape while simultaneously pairing edge-corner blocks. Unlike Vandenbergh where cubeshape is purely about restoring the shape, the Lin method aims to create useful blocks during this phase. This requires deeper understanding of how pieces move during slash moves.
tips: Plan blocks during inspection — this is the hardest part
Understand how slash moves affect both shape and piece permutation
Practice recognizing which blocks can be formed during cubeshape
Color neutrality helps find easier block-building opportunities

steps.name: CSP (Cubeshape Parity)
steps.description: Handle the cubeshape parity situation. Parity on Square-1 is determined during cubeshape, and the Lin method addresses it explicitly at this stage rather than deferring it to the end. This step may involve specific algorithms or adjustments depending on the parity state.
tips: Some cubeshape solutions inherently avoid or resolve parity
Parity-aware cubeshape planning is a key skill

steps.name: Solve Last Layer
steps.description: Solve the remaining pieces of the last layer. Because blocks were paired during cubeshape, the last layer has fewer pieces to solve. This step uses algorithms to orient and permute the remaining corners and edges in as few looks as possible.
tips: Recognition is different from Vandenbergh due to pre-paired blocks
Practice the unique last layer cases that arise from the Lin approach
Aim to reduce looks by combining orientation and permutation steps

pros: Lower overall move count than Vandenbergh
Fewer total steps when blocks are paired efficiently
Parity is handled earlier, reducing end-of-solve complexity
Rewards strong planning and inspection skills
cons: Much harder inspection planning than Vandenbergh
Blockbuilding during cubeshape is difficult to master
Less structured than Vandenbergh, requiring more flexibility
